rxn_class_summary: Enantioselective electrochemical Rh-catalyzed dehydrogenation/conjugate addition of imidazolyl alkyl ketones with dienes (and Michael acceptors) via an α,β-unsaturated ketone (alkene) intermediate and Rh-enolate.
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation (mediated by ferrocene) of the Rh-bound enolate/substrate drives α,β-dehydrogenation of the alkyl ketone to generate an electrophilic enone intermediate that is subsequently captured by a nucleophile (diene or enolate addition) under chiral Rh control.
electron_flow: Λ-RhS coordinates the imidazolyl ketone and forms a Rh-enolate (assisted by DABCO); ferrocene is oxidized at the RVC anode to ferrocenium, which performs SET on the Rh-enolate to give an α-carbon radical that loses a second electron/proton to deliver the α,β-unsaturated ketone (52). This electrophilic enone is then trapped enantioselectively by a Rh-enolate of a second ketone or by a diene/Michael acceptor; protons are reduced to H2 at the Pt cathode.
rate_determining_step: Anodic α,β-dehydrogenation of the Rh-enolate to give the enone intermediate (formation of 52)

probe_reagents.name: 2,3-dimethyl-1,3-butadiene (2a)
probe_reagents.observation: Omitted under standard conditions: the alkene intermediate 52 was isolated in 72% yield, confirming enone forms before diene engagement.

probe_reagents.name: no light control (without irradiation)
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction without light still delivers 52 in 71% yield, indicating the dehydrogenation step is electrochemical, not photochemical.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: α-aminoxy adduct 53 isolated in 8% (23% ee) along with 86% of enone 52, supporting a transient α-carbon radical intermediate during dehydrogenation.

probe_reagents.name: phenyl vinyl ketone (Michael acceptor)
probe_reagents.observation: Under non-electrochemical conditions with Λ-RhS/DABCO, conjugate addition product 55 forms in 96% yield, 97% ee, demonstrating that the Rh-enolate is the nucleophilic species responsible for enantiocontrol.

notes: Two key mechanistic conclusions: (i) the catalytic cycle proceeds through an isolable α,β-unsaturated ketone (52) generated by anodic dehydrogenation rather than by direct radical coupling, and (ii) enantioselectivity arises in the Rh-enolate Michael addition step (demonstrated by the non-electrochemical enolate trapping with phenyl vinyl ketone giving 97% ee). The low ee (23%) of the TEMPO adduct 53 suggests TEMPO traps a relatively free α-radical off the chiral Rh, consistent with an off-pathway/secondary capture rather than the productive enantiodetermining step.
